narrative_summary:
The POLE p.Ala252Val (A252V) missense change has been reported across a range of tumor types, most often as an incidental finding rather than a driver alteration. In a cohort of 52 sporadic colorectal cancers the variant was present in ten tumours at a frequency that did not differ from that observed in the general population, suggesting it is a common polymorphism rather than a tumour‑specific event [PMC4310866]. A phase‑II study of toripalimab identified a single patient with a germline A252V mutation but did not provide any variant‑specific efficacy or outcome data [PMC11366758]. A review of clinical outcomes linked the same change to reduced overall survival in lung cancer patients, implying a possible adverse effect in that context [PMC2993855]. Conversely, large case‑control analyses of breast cancer susceptibility found no significant association between A252V and disease risk [PMC4204770], and a gastric‑cancer polymorphism study likewise reported no link to cancer susceptibility [PMC5482622]. The variant also appears at a relatively high frequency (≈10 % of tumours) in a Chinese non‑small‑cell lung‑cancer cohort, again without functional or prognostic annotation [PMC8787147].

Functional investigations are limited. In human induced‑pluripotent stem‑cell lines the A252V substitution was classified as a high‑ or moderate‑impact missense mutation, yet the engineered cells did not develop malignant phenotypes, indicating that the change alone may be insufficient to drive transformation [PMC6171902]. In the NCI‑60 drug‑response panel the variant was associated with altered sensitivity to certain natural products, suggesting a measurable biological effect on drug response [PMC7858122]. Computational predictions provide mixed signals: one analysis reported deleterious SIFT scores (0.01) and probably damaging PolyPhen‑2 scores, supporting a functional impact [PMC11312386], whereas another clinical genetics report listed the variant as benign based on existing annotations [PMC6439399]. Additional sequencing panels have recorded A252V as a variant of unknown significance in endometrial cancer and other tumour types, reflecting uncertainty about its clinical relevance [PMC7706212].

Population data reinforce the notion that A252V is a relatively common allele. The colorectal cancer series showed an allele frequency comparable to that in public databases (approximately 16 % in the general population) [PMC4310866], and the breast‑cancer consortium similarly found no enrichment of the variant among cases versus controls [PMC4204770]. The NSCLC cohort reported a 10.6 % prevalence among tumours, a frequency that is compatible with a common polymorphism rather than a rare pathogenic mutation [PMC8787147].

Taken together, the evidence is conflicting. While some clinical observations (reduced lung‑cancer survival) and in silico predictions suggest a deleterious effect, the majority of population‑based studies show a high background frequency and lack of disease association, and functional assays have not demonstrated oncogenic transformation. The variant is therefore best interpreted as a common polymorphism of uncertain clinical significance, with limited and inconsistent data to support a pathogenic role.

mentions.quoted_text: Inspired by the association of Polε exonuclease domain mutations with intestinal cancer in mice (2) and humans (5, 8, 9), we began this study by analyzing this region of Polε in 52 sporadic CRC patients treated at UNMC. The age distribution of the patients and the anatomical site of the tumor are listed in Table 1. Ten of the 52 tumors carried a common variant allele producing an **Ala252Val** change in Polε (nine in heterozygous and one in homozygous state; Table 1). The variant allele frequency did not differ significantly (p=0.11; Chi-square) from that reported for the general population (16). One tumor carried an Arg259His variant, also observed previously in the general population (allele frequency 0.5-1%). Remarkably, one tumor (T-04-364) had a heterozygous mutation leading to a P286R substitution in the ExoI motif (Table 1; Fig. 1). This change was not present in the publicly available databases of common polymorphisms, but was reported previously as a somatic mutation in 16 other sporadic cancer cases (6, 7, 9) and as a potential cancer-specific variant in a colon carcinoma cell line HCC2998 (10). Other mutations affecting Pro286 in Polε or a homologous Pro327 in Polδ were also observed in CRC patients (5, 8). The P286R change found in tumor T-04-364 was not present in normal cells of the patient (Fig. 1A), indicating that it was a somatic mutation. The recurrent emergence of Pro286 alterations is in sharp contrast to the apparently random incidence in tumors of other changes in the exonuclease domain of Polε, most of which were only observed once (5-7, 9).

article_mentions.overall_article_summary: The article notes that the POLE A252V variant is found in a subset of colorectal tumors at a frequency comparable to the general population, offering no direct evidence of pathogenicity for this variant.
article_mentions.title: A common cancer-associated DNA polymerase ε mutation causes an exceptionally strong mutator phenotype, indicating fidelity defects distinct from loss of proofreading

mentions.quoted_text: POLE/POLD1 variants are detailed in Table 2 and Fig. 3. Of all 15 patients, ten had POLE mutation (one **A252V** variant was germline), four had POLD1 mutation (one R682Q variant was germline), and one had malignant peripheral nerve sheath tumor with both POLE and POLD1 mutations. Most patients had one POLE or POLD1 mutation, while three had more than one mutation: one with S297F and S1906Y POLE mutations, one with P286R and F1907L POLE mutations, one with L523-L526del POLD1 mutation, I2070-K2072del and T2273-E2275del POLE mutations. Among three patients with more than one mutation, two had an objective response, and one did not undergo a CT scan after one dose of toripalimab.

article_mentions.overall_article_summary: The phase‑II trial reports overall response rates for POLE/POLD1‑mutated tumours but provides only a generic clinical observation that A252V is a germline POLE mutation in one patient, without variant‑specific efficacy data.
article_mentions.title: A phase II clinical trial of toripalimab in advanced solid tumors with polymerase epsilon/polymerase delta (POLE/POLD1) mutation

mentions.quoted_text: There are relatively few studies examining the cancer risk associated with non-synonymous SNPS in POLD1 or POLE. POLE E2140K has been associated with an increased risk of breast cancer [207], but not cervical cancer [208]; **A252V** is associated with decreased overall survival in lung cancer patients [209]; and N1396S is not associated with meningioma risk [210]. The POLD1 R119H SNP has been reported in multiple genome-wide SNP studies, and no association was detected with meningioma, bladder or breast cancer risk [210–213]. Surprisingly, most studies looking for associations with increased cancer risk have studied POLD1 or POLE intronic or synonymous coding SNPs that are unlikely to affect polymerase expression or fidelity.

article_mentions.overall_article_summary: The review notes that POLE A252V is associated with reduced overall survival in lung cancer, suggesting a potentially deleterious clinical effect.
article_mentions.title: DNA Replication Fidelity and Cancer

article_mentions.title: Utility of a custom designed next generation DNA sequencing gene panel to molecularly classify endometrial cancers according to The Cancer Genome Atlas subgroups

article_mentions.overall_article_summary: The study examined 41 common nsSNPs for breast cancer risk and found that POLE A252V showed no significant association with disease susceptibility.
article_mentions.title: Common non-synonymous SNPs associated with breast cancer susceptibility: findings from the Breast Cancer Association Consortium

article_mentions.overall_article_summary: The study identifies POLE A252V as a high/moderate impact missense mutation in hiPSC lines but observes no malignant tumors, indicating the variant did not drive malignancy in this context.
article_mentions.title: Tumorigenicity-associated characteristics of human iPS cell lines

article_mentions.overall_article_summary: The article documents the identification of the POLE p.A252V variant in circulating tumor DNA but does not provide any interpretation of its pathogenicity or clinical relevance.
article_mentions.title: Combining germline, tissue and liquid biopsy analysis by comprehensive genomic profiling to improve the yield of actionable variants in a real-world cancer cohort

article_mentions.overall_article_summary: The study examined numerous DNA‑repair gene polymorphisms for gastric‑cancer susceptibility, but it does not report any association or interpretation for the POLE A252V variant.
article_mentions.title: Relevance of DNA repair gene polymorphisms to gastric cancer risk and phenotype

article_mentions.overall_article_summary: The paper introduces an adaptive mutation‑specific negative (MSN) method for more accurate classification of negative versus unknown mutation status in multi‑sample NGS data and reports somatic variants such as POLE A252V in single‑cell sequencing tables.
article_mentions.title: An adaptive method of defining negative mutation status for multi-sample comparison using next-generation sequencing

article_mentions.overall_article_summary: The study evaluates different tumor sampling strategies for whole‑exome sequencing, concluding that spatially adjacent biopsies give similar mutation profiles and that larger pooled samples may dilute sub‑clonal mutations in hypermutated tumors, without focusing on the POLE A252V variant itself.
article_mentions.title: Currently favored sampling practices for tumor sequencing can produce optimal results in the clinical setting
